podcasts.name: Moody's Podcasts
podcasts.title: Meet Moody’s AI Agents
podcasts.excerpt: AI agents are rapidly shifting how businesses approach automation and decision-making. As capabilities evolve, organizations are rethinking workflows, data, and the role of human expertise alongside intelligent systems. In this episode, Olga Loiseau-Aslanidi , Managing Director for AI Solutions and Keith Berry , General Manager at Moody’s, join host Alex Pillow to explore how AI agents are transforming risk, compliance, and operational efficiency. They discuss how agentic systems combine large language models with tools, data, and workflows to move beyond chat-based interactions and help actively complete complex tasks. Key topics covered: What AI agents are and how they differ from traditional chatbots The concept of “harnessing” models with tools, data, and guardrails to drive outcomes Real-world use cases, including screening, due diligence, and entity enrichment How organizations balance automation with human oversight in regulated environments Why trusted, decision-grade data is critical to effective AI-enabled outcomes How firms might adopt agentic solutions based on their risk appetite and maturity Additional resources: Moody’s agentic solutions landing page Blogs: Why skills decide who controls AI in financial services ; Where enterprise AI actually gets built ; Decision-grade intelligence in an AI-driven world To learn more about Moody’s please visit our website or get in touch — we would love to hear from you. Hosted by Simplecast, an AdsWizz company. See pcm.adswizz.com for information about our collection and use of personal data for advertising.

This is Risk Reframed, the show, where we bring you the leading risk experts from Moody's, our partners and the wider ecosystem to discuss the risks organizations face, how they're interconnected, and ultimately what you, the risk professional, can do about them. Every conversation we have on Risk Reframe these days at some point comes onto AI agents and what comes next. Like most businesses in this space, Moody's has been busy working with customers on what to build and launch in terms of agents. And we're going to unpack some of that today and meet some of Moody's agents themselves. We'll do that with my colleagues, Olga and Keith Berry. What you were saying, asking question and getting answer. What agent is, is really delegating work that you want to get done. So really giving it an objective, giving it a set of tools, giving it really a way how to do things, setting guardrails and then get things done. So that's really what what agent is. So it still uses the large language model like ChatGPT as Brain, but then it can actually do stuff, not just tell us, okay, go and do this. Okay, so if the LLM is the brain. Yeah. Would do work to achieve objective that we give it. Okay, so these are the component parts that are the how it works, Right? Exactly. And a lot of people say harness and I don't know, sometimes I understand it and I forget it and I hear it again. How does the harness work? Yeah. So why people started talking about harness is because when you just look at large language model alone, it's almost never enough for the thing that you want to do. So you want to bring the whole ecosystem or if you want harness. So if you think about large language model as your cpu, harness is kind of operating system around it. So when you give an agent tools, when you give connection through for example, what is called model context protocols, when you give it access to Data to your APIs, you do evaluations. So all these components of the ecosystem represent hardness. Okay. So it's a nice sort of collective term for all of it that's making it work. Exactly. Keep that in mind as we go through today. Keith, you've been a technologist your whole career. What harness do we need to build in order to solve that particular problem or a use case? Yeah, I mean, the job to be done I don't think is changing. The way you might achieve that job might be different. So, I mean, maybe to explain a bit of the story behind the screening agent, I mean, that came out of a customer who were a crypto firm who were moving very fast. And we really started with that back in pretty early, back in 2023, I think towards the end of 2023 with actually a discussion over dinner with their chief compliance officer who was talking about the spikes they were seeing from actually all the sanctions that were coming out with the Russia Ukraine war and how that was really impacting their analysts. So it's really frictionless exercise. So it's not agent per agent, it's really kind of building blocks. Where does it go, what does it interact with, et cetera. Exactly. And I know we talk a lot about the data as well in terms of obviously the cliche of garbage in, garbage out. I won't accuse others of having garbage, but what goes in matters. Keith, could you talk about that a little bit? Yeah. I think at the heart of everything that's happening with AI is the need for really good quality data, or decision grade data as we've started to call it at boonies and especially in these kind of mission critical use cases that we operate in. Right. If somebody makes a mistake in the world of kyc, then there's potential financial repercussions, reputational repercussions. So thinking about the trusted data that goes into that's really important and I think that's one of the things that makes us unique is that we have this large data estate of, of millions of records, but millions of records on companies and individuals associated with companies and the facilities those companies work out of and everything from that to cyber events and other sorts of risk events, extreme weather events for example, and how we join all those up into a knowledge graph. Now the Power of that is you can access that information very quickly and easily because we've structured that data and joined it up. And that's really at the heart of what differentiates us and what we do. I think we often hear, well, could you just use an LLM, an AI, to go and find some of that data? And there are elements of that data where that is true. But I think as organizations are starting to understand, if you have an AI, go and scrape the data across the web, there's a cost to that. And if you're doing that every time you're looking up the name of a company, there's a cost to it, then do you trust it? There's been a couple of stories in the last few weeks about people planting fake research, really for the purpose of demonstrating that you can't always believe what an AI is going to bring back. And so, you know, having data that's trusted, having data that's joined, joined up, and the joining up of those data sets is one of the unique challenges and kind of the validation that we put into our data becomes very powerful. I don't know who wants to take it first, but yeah, maybe I can start.
